ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class; no specific torikoku (chicken bone broth) ramen with egg subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific torikoku ramen entry. A00GJ (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for torikoku ramen with egg as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of torikoku ramen with egg weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, noodles, egg, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup with chicken broth and egg — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken broth, wheat ramen noodles (cooked), hard-boiled egg, and standard ramen toppings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for torikoku ramen with egg. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for: cooked wheat ramen noodles (FDC 168936), chicken broth (FDC 171950), hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424), and minor toppings (bamboo shoots, nori, green onion). Proportions based on a standard 500 g bowl: approximately 200 g broth, 150 g cooked noodles, 60 g egg, 90 g toppings. Per-100 g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, wheat-based — GI reference; wheat noodle soup GI analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for torikoku ramen with egg. GI estimated at 55 based on University of Sydney GI Database data for wheat-based noodle soups and cooked wheat noodles, which range from 47–61 (medium GI). The protein and fat content of the broth and egg moderately attenuate glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 60) / 100 = 33.0, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving ≈ 60 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from chicken broth and soy sauce tare
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl of torikoku ramen with egg provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which substantially exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg and should be consumed only occasionally by individuals with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodles with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of 33 per bowl, torikoku ramen with egg poses a moderate glycemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from egg yolk and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 4 g per bowl) in torikoku ramen with egg are relevant risk factors for cardiovascular disease, and individuals should limit frequency of consumption and consider requesting reduced-sodium broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Torikoku ramen with egg (鶏骨ラーメン卵入り) is a Japanese noodle soup dish featuring a rich, opaque chicken bone broth (torikoku, meaning 'chicken richness') served with wheat ramen noodles, a soft-boiled or marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), and typical toppings such as nori (dried seaweed), bamboo shoots (menma), and sliced green onion. The broth is prepared by simmering chicken carcasses and bones for several hours to extract collagen and fat, producing a creamy, full-bodied soup that is milder and less fatty than tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen. The dish is described as non-spicy, making it accessible to a wide range of palates. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 475 kcal, 29 g protein, 14 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of around 2100 mg. The medium glycemic index (approximately 55) reflects the wheat noodle base. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and should be consumed with caution by individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The egg contributes high-quality protein and micronutrients including vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline. This dish is popular in Japanese ramen restaurants in Singapore, including at Ramen Matsuri.
common_user_queries: Calories in chicken ramen with egg
Japanese ramen with soft boiled egg

ingredient_synonyms.torikoku: chicken bone broth, chicken paitan, tori paitan
ingredient_synonyms.tamago: egg, soft-boiled egg, marinated egg, ajitsuke tamago, ramen egg

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for torikoku ramen with egg as a specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for chicken broth, cooked wheat ramen noodles, hard-boiled egg, and minor toppings. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for wheat noodle soups. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the proportions used reflect a realistic standard bowl.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single approved-source record for torikoku ramen with egg exists. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as chicken broth and wheat noodles contain negligible industrially produced trans fats per USDA SR Legacy data. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; positive value estimated from component data). 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle soup data; no direct measurement for torikoku ramen exists. 5. Sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g; ~2100 mg per 500 g bowl) due to soy sauce tare and chicken broth; this is the primary clinical concern. 6. The input food_group was listed as 'Dairy and eggs' which is incorrect for a noodle soup dish; corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite of noodles, broth, protein, and toppings. 7. is_halal set to false as standard ramen broth preparation and soy sauce tare may contain non-halal ingredients; halal certification status for Ramen Matsuri Singapore is not confirmed in approved sources. 8. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the component-level USDA SR Legacy estimation approach used; these are not confirmed zeros.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is canonical English. Japanese local name 鶏骨ラーメン（卵入り）is present satisfying G6 for Japanese cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with explicit component estimation methodology documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.6), sodium_mg (420.0), saturated_fat_g (0.8), and sugar_g (0.8) are all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55, numeric > 0), gi_category (medium), glycemic_load_per_serving (33.0), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate with documented basis.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent (FOODON_00002708, ramen noodle soup) and FoodEx2 closest_parent (A00GJ, Noodle soup) are defensible. SNOMED CT is none with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, GI/GL), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, sodium and saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list is comprehensive with English variants, Japanese script, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is consistent with flat aliases list. All strings in aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array.

Verified trans_fat_g zero is documented. Verified GI is numeric > 0. Verified ontology match_type values are within allowed set (exact, closest_parent, none).
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all six hard gates pass and all eight Q criteria score 1/1 for a total of 8/8. The record is a composite estimate from USDA SR Legacy component data with medium confidence, appropriate for a restaurant dish not present in any single approved primary source. Japanese multilingual naming is satisfied. Aliases are consistent between aliases_by_language and flat aliases. Sodium is flagged as the primary clinical concern.
